reason: The item cleanly tests boundary-aware admissibility: readers must separate (i) SOC’s effect on propagation intensity from (ii) runaway possibility, and prioritize shell-mediated heat transfer over pole-connector pathways. Decoys are largely single-fault and engineering-plausible, though some use conspicuous absolutes (“cannot,” “fully prevent”) that slightly cue elimination and reduce discriminative strength for experts who can quickly pattern-match overreach.

The prompt tightly constrains the respondent to a specific mechanistic stage (around 100°C), an evidence-based statement about outcome certainty at ~90% SOC without cooling, and a timing-based interpretation of water spray effectiveness (prevention vs suppression), yielding clear boundary-aware discrimination. It should separate experts from heuristic answers because it penalizes “wait-and-see” reasoning and demands linking surface temperature to internal exothermic processes and intervention role. Minor neutrality risk remains because the narrative framing (“engineer proposes… evaluate”) can cue the expected stance even though the required justification is still technically demanding.
